title: Tournedos Rossini
subtitle: beef fillet medallion on a croûton with a Périgueux sauce
excerpt: Tournedos Rossini is a classic French restaurant dish: a thick medallion of beef fillet pan-fried and served on a fried bread croûton, topped with foie gras and finished with a Madeira and truffle sauce. The components are rich and the dish is one of the grandest in the French classical repertoire.
sourceNotes: Auguste Escoffier, Le Guide Culinaire (1903), public domain. Tournedos Rossini method, Sauce Périgueux composition, croûton preparation. The dish is attributed to the chef Casimir Moisson at the Café Anglais in Paris and named for the Italian composer Gioachino Rossini, a regular patron in the 1860s. Marie-Antoine Carême, L'Art de la Cuisine Française (1833), public domain. Tournedos and beef fillet principles.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: The Périgueux sauce can be made up to 1 hour ahead. The croûtons can be fried 30 minutes ahead and kept warm in a low oven.

glossaryTerms.definition: A small, thick medallion cut from the centre of a beef fillet, typically about 2.5 to 3 cm thick and 6 to 7 cm across, tied with string to hold its shape during cooking.
A classical French sauce made from a demi-glace base with Madeira wine and truffle, named after the city of Périgueux in the Dordogne, a region famous for truffles and foie gras.
In classical French cooking, a croûton can refer to a round or shaped piece of bread fried in butter until golden and crisp, used as a base for meat or garnishes.

content.text: Tournedos Rossini is one of the set pieces of French classical cooking. A
tournedos
of beef fillet sits on a fried bread
croûton

, is topped with a slice of foie gras, and is finished with a Madeira sauce that has a few shavings of truffle stirred through it. The whole thing is rich, theatrical, and not especially complicated once you have the sauce made.

Foie gras is not in the ingredient list. Use one slice of pan-seared duck or goose liver per portion, placed on top of the tournedos before serving. If foie gras is unavailable, a thick slice of chicken liver pâté at room temperature on the croûton works well. For the
Périgueux sauce
, fresh truffle is ideal but truffle paste is widely sold and gives a good result.
What you need

items.prepNote: cut into 2 medallions, each about 2.5 cm thick, tied with string
thick slices, cut into rounds the same diameter as the tournedos

content.text: You will also need about 80 g of foie gras (fresh, sliced, or from a terrine) per person, or a generous spoonful of good chicken liver pâté as an alternative. Neither is in the ingredient lookup; both are used in the traditional manner described below.
Method
Make the Périgueux sauce
In a small saucepan, cook the {{shallot}} chopped shallots in a knob of butter over medium heat for 3 minutes until soft. Add the {{fortified-madeira}} of Madeira and
reduce

by two-thirds, about 5 minutes. Add the {{stock-beef}} of beef stock and reduce again until the sauce coats the back of a spoon, another 8 to 10 minutes. Season, then stir in the {{truffle-paste}} of truffle paste. Keep warm over very low heat. Just before serving, whisk in the cold butter off the heat to give the sauce a gloss.
Fry the croûtons

Melt {{unsalted-butter}} of butter in a frying pan over medium heat. Add the {{bread}} bread rounds and fry for 2 minutes per side until golden and crisp. Remove to a warm plate and keep in a low oven while you cook the beef.
Cook the tournedos
Season the {{beef-fillet}} of beef fillet medallions with salt and pepper. Heat {{olive-oil}} of olive oil in a clean frying pan over very high heat.
Sear

the tournedos for 2 minutes per side until deeply browned. Add {{unsalted-butter}} of butter and baste for 1 minute. The target for medium-rare is 54°C internal; check with a probe. Rest for 5 minutes on a warm plate before assembling.
Assemble and serve

Place a croûton on each warm plate. Remove the string from each tournedos and set it on the croûton. If using foie gras, heat a dry pan until very hot and sear the slices for 30 seconds per side, then place on top of the beef. Spoon the Périgueux sauce around and over the top. Serve immediately.

items.cause: String not tied tightly enough before cooking
items.fix: Tie kitchen string around the circumference of each medallion before cooking; this keeps the shape under heat

items.cause: Beef stock was too light, or the Madeira was not reduced enough
items.fix: Use a good-quality reduced stock; the Madeira must reduce by two-thirds before the stock goes in to concentrate the flavour
Always assemble just before serving; the croûton absorbs the meat juices quickly

items.cause: Pan was too hot or the medallions were thin
items.fix: Check the internal temperature with a probe from the side rather than the top; thin medallions cook in under 2 minutes per side

content.text: Where this dish lives

Tournedos Rossini belongs to the grand-restaurant tradition of Paris in the second half of the nineteenth century. It combines three ingredients that were considered the height of luxury at the time: beef fillet, foie gras, and truffle. The dish was codified by Escoffier and became a staple of formal French dining menus. At home it is most suited to a very small dinner party where the components can be assembled quickly. Serve alone on the plate with the sauce; the dish needs nothing else.
